sections.title: 1.1 The distinction between audit and review of financial information
paragraphs: An audit is a form of assurance engagement. The objective of an audit is to enable the auditor to form an opinion as to whether the financial statements of an entity give a ‘true and fair view’.
An audit is designed to provide a high level of assurance to the users of the financial statements. Review of financial information is done to:
a) obtain limited assurance, primarily by performing inquiry and analytical procedures in accordance with applicable financial reporting framework; and
b) report on the financial statements.

sections.title: 1.2 Review engagement
paragraphs: Unless required by law or regulation, the practitioner shall not accept a review engagement if:
* The practitioner is not satisfied that:
- there is a rational purpose for the engagement
- a review engagement would be appropriate in the circumstances
* The Practitioner has reason to believe that relevant ethical requirements, including independence will not be satisfied.
* The Practitioner has doubt over management integrity.

sections.title: Precondition for accepting a Review Engagement
paragraphs: * Whether financial reporting framework is acceptable
* Obtain agreement of management that it acknowledge and understand that it has the responsibility for:
- the preparation of financial statements in accordance with approved financial reporting framework
- internal controls
- providing practitioner with all information

sections.title: 1.3 Engagement and performance
paragraphs: The practitioner shall agree the terms of the engagement with management or those charged with governance that shall include:
a) The intended use and distribution of the financial statements,
b) Identification of the applicable financial reporting framework;
c) The objective and scope of the review engagement;
d) The responsibilities of the practitioner;
e) The responsibilities of management.;
f) A statement that the engagement is not an audit, and that the practitioner will not express an audit opinion on the financial statements; and
g) Reference to the expected form and content of the report to be issued by the practitioner.

sections.title: Performing the Engagement
paragraphs: The practitioner shall determine and apply materiality for the financial statements as a whole. The practitioner shall obtain an understanding of the entity and its environment.
In obtaining sufficient appropriate evidence the practitioner shall design and perform inquiry and analytical procedures.

sections.title: Inquiry
paragraphs: Inquiries of management and others within the entity shall include inquiries relating to:
a) how management makes significant accounting estimates.
b) the identification of related parties and related party transactions and their purpose

c) whether there are significant, unusual or complex transactions, events or matters including significant changes in the entity's business activity or operations, significant adjustments, significant transactions occurring near the end of the reporting period, the existence of any frauds or illegal acts or non-compliance, subsequent events, going concern, material commitments etc.
d) the existence of any actual, suspected or alleged fraud
e) whether management has identified or addressed subsequent events
f) the basis for management assessment for going concern
g) whether there are events or condition that may cast significant doubt on the entity’s ability to continue as a going concern
If the practitioner identifies significant transactions outside the entity's normal course of business, the practitioner shall inquire of management about:
a) the nature of those transactions;
b) whether related parties could be involved; and
c) the business rationale (or lack thereof) of those transactions.

sections.title: Going concern
paragraphs: A review of financial statements includes consideration of the entity's ability to continue as a going concern. In this consideration, the practitioner shall cover the same period as that used by management.

sections.title: Use of work performed by others
paragraphs: If the practitioner uses work performed by another practitioner or an expert in the course of performing the review, the practitioner shall take appropriate steps to be satisfied that the work performed is adequate for the practitioner's purposes.

sections.title: Subsequent Events
paragraphs: If the practitioner becomes aware of subsequent events that require adjustment of, or disclosure in. the financial statements the practitioner shall request management to correct those misstatements.

sections.title: Written Representations
paragraphs: The practitioner shall request management to provide a written representation that management has fulfilled its responsibilities described in the agreed terms of engagement.

The practitioner shall also request management’s written representations that management has disclosed to the practitioner entity's related parties and related party transactions, significant facts relating to any frauds, known non-compliance, all information relevant to going concern assumption, subsequent events, material commitments and material non-monetary transactions

sections.title: 1.4 Forming the Practitioner’s conclusion on the Financial Statements
paragraphs: In forming the conclusion on the financial statements, the practitioner shall:
a) evaluate whether the financial statements adequately refer to or describe the applicable financial reporting framework
b) consider whether in the context of the requirements of the applicable financial reporting framework and the results of procedures performed:
i. The terminology used in the financial statements including the title of each financial statement is appropriate;
ii. The financial statements adequately disclose the significant accounting policies selected and applied;
iii. The accounting policies selected and applied are consistent with the applicable financial reporting framework and are appropriate;
iv. Accounting estimates made by management appear reasonable;
v. The information presented in the financial statements appears relevant, reliable, comparable, and understandable; and
vi. The financial statements provide adequate disclosures.

sections.title: Unmodified Conclusion
paragraphs: The practitioner shall express an unmodified conclusion in the practitioner's report on the financial statements as a whole when the practitioner has obtained limited assurance to be able to conclude that nothing has come to the practitioner's attention that causes the practitioner to believe that the financial statements are not prepared, in all material respects, in accordance with the applicable financial reporting framework.

sections.title: Modified Conclusion
paragraphs: The practitioner shall express a modified conclusion in the practitioner's report on the financial statements as a whole when:
a) The practitioner determines, that the financial statements are materially misstated; or
b) The practitioner is unable to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence.
When the practitioner modifies the conclusion expressed on the financial statements, the practitioner shall:
a) use the heading "Qualified Conclusion," "Adverse Conclusion" or "Disclaimer of Conclusion," as appropriate, for the conclusion paragraph in the practitioner's report; and
b) provide a description of the matter giving rise to the modification.

sections.title: Financial statements are materially misstated
paragraphs: If the practitioner determines that the financial statements are materially misstated, the practitioner shall express:
a) A qualified conclusion, when the practitioner concludes that the effects of the matter(s) giving rise to the modification are material, but not pervasive to the financial statements; or
b) An adverse conclusion, when the effects of the matter(s) giving rise to the modification are both material and pervasive to the financial statements.

In the basis for conclusion paragraph, in relation to material misstatements that give rise to either a qualified conclusion or an adverse conclusion, the practitioner shall describe and quantify the financial effects of the misstatement if the material misstatement relates to specific accounts in the financial statements (including quantitative disclosures), unless impracticable, in which case the practitioner shall also explain:
i. how disclosures are misstated; or
ii. the nature of omitted information.

sections.title: Inability to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence
paragraphs: If the practitioner is unable to form a conclusion on the financial statements due to inability to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence the practitioner shall:
a) Express a qualified conclusion if the practitioner concludes that the possible effects on the financial statements of undetected misstatements, if any, could be material but not pervasive; or
b) Disclaim a conclusion if the practitioner concludes that the possible effects on the financial statements of undetected misstatements, if any, could be both material and pervasive.
The practitioner shall disclaim a conclusion on the financial statements, or withdraw from the engagement, if:
a) The practitioner concludes that there is sufficient doubt about the integrity of management; or
b) Management does not provide the representations that are the preconditions for accepting an engagement.

sections.title: The Practitioner’s Report
paragraphs: The practitioner's report for the review engagement shall be in writing, and shall contain the following elements:
a) A title of report
b) The addressee(s),
c) An introductory paragraph that:
i. identifies the financial statements reviewed, including identification of the title of each of the statements contained;
ii. refers to the summary of significant accounting policies and other explanatory information; and
iii. states that the financial statements have been reviewed;
d) A description of the responsibility of management for the preparation of the financial statements.
e) A description of the practitioner's responsibility to express a conclusion on the financial statements including reference to this ISRE and, where relevant, applicable law or regulation;
f) A description of a review of financial statements and its limitations, and the following statements:
i. a review engagement under this ISRE is a limited assurance engagement;

ii. the practitioner performs procedures, primarily consisting of making inquiries of management and others within the entity, as appropriate, and applying analytical procedures, and evaluates the evidence obtained; and

iii. the procedures performed in a review are substantially less than those performed in an audit conducted in accordance with International Standards on Auditing (ISAs), and, accordingly, the practitioner does not express an audit opinion on the financial statements;
g) A paragraph under the heading "Conclusion" that contains:
i. the practitioner conclusion on the financial statements as a whole
ii. a reference to the applicable financial reporting framework used to prepare the financial statements,
iii. when the practitioner’s conclusion on the financial statements is modified:
h) A paragraph under the appropriate heading that contains the practitioner's modified conclusion
i) A paragraph, under an appropriate heading, that provides a description of the matter(s) giving rise to the modification
j) A reference to the practitioner's obligation under this ISRE to comply with relevant ethical requirements
k) Date of the practitioner's report
l) Practitioner's signature
m) Location in the jurisdiction where the practitioner practices

sections.title: Emphasis of Matter Paragraph
paragraphs: The practitioner may consider it necessary to draw users' attention to a matter presented or disclosed in the financial statements. In such cases, the practitioner shall include an Emphasis of Matter paragraph in the practitioner's report.

sections.title: 2.1 Definition of assurance
paragraphs: ‘Assurance’ means confidence. In an assurance engagement, an ‘assurance firm’ is engaged by one party to give an opinion on a piece of information that has been prepared by another party. The opinion is an expression of assurance about the information that has been reviewed. It gives assurance to the party that hired the assurance firm that the information can be relied on.
As per the study text area covered till this point, what we have learned is that, assurance can be provided by:
* audit: this may be external audit, internal audit or a combination of the two
* review.

A statutory audit is one form of assurance. Without assurance from the auditors, the shareholders may not accept that the information provided by the financial statements is sufficiently accurate and reliable. The statutory audit provides assurance as to the quality of the information.
The provision of this assurance should add credibility to the information in the financial statements, making the information more reliable and therefore more useful to the user.
However, there are differing levels or degrees of assurance. Some assurances are more reliable than others.

sections.title: 2.2 Levels of assurance
paragraphs: The degree of assurance that can be provided about the reliability of the financial statements of a company will depend on:
* the amount of work performed in carrying out the assurance process, and
* the results of that work.
The resulting assurance falls into one of two categories:

* Reasonable Assurance – A high (but not absolute) level of assurance provided by the practitioner’s conclusion expressed in a positive form. E.g. “In our opinion the accounts are true and fair”. The objective of a statutory audit is to provide reasonable assurance.

* Limited Assurance – A moderate level of assurance provided by the practitioner’s conclusion expressed in a negative form. E.g. “Based on our review, nothing has come to our attention that causes us to believe that the accompanying financial statements do not give a true and fair view”. The objective of a review engagement is often to provide limited assurance.

sections.title: Assurance provided by audit
paragraphs: An audit provides a high, but not absolute, level of assurance that the audited information is free from any material misstatement. This is often referred to as reasonable assurance.

The reason why auditor is not expected to provide absolute assurance is that there are inherent limitations of an audit, which result in most of the audit evidence on which the auditor draws conclusions and bases the auditor’s opinion being persuasive rather than conclusive. The inherent limitations of an audit are discussed later in this chapter.

sections.title: Assurance provided by review
paragraphs: A review in contrast to ‘reasonable’ level of assurance provided by an audit, a review into an aspect of the financial statements would provide only a moderate level of assurance that the information under review is free of material misstatement. The resulting opinion is usually (although not always) expressed in the form of negative assurance.
Negative assurance is an opinion that nothing is obviously wrong: in other words, ‘nothing has come to our attention to suggest that the information is misstated’.

A review does not provide the same amount of assurance as an audit. An external audit provides positive assurance that, in the opinion of the auditors, the financial statements do present fairly the financial position and performance of the company.
The higher level of assurance provided by an audit will enhance the credibility provided by the assurance process, but the audit work is likely to be:
* more time-consuming than a review, and so
* costlier than a review.

sections.title: 2.3 Elements of an assurance engagement
paragraphs: ⯈ Definition: Assurance engagement

An engagement in which a practitioner aims to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence in order to express a conclusion designed to enhance the degree of confidence of the intended users other than the responsible party about the subject matter information (that is, the outcome of the measurement or evaluation of an underlying subject matter against criteria).
An assurance engagement performed by a practitioner will consists of the following five elements:
* A three party relationship:
- Practitioner – the individual providing professional services that will review the subject matter and provide the assurance. E.g. the audit firm in a statutory audit
- Responsible party – the person(s) responsible for the subject matter. E.g. the Directors are responsible for preparing the financial statements to be audited
- Intended users – the person(s) or class of persons for whom the practitioner prepares the assurance report. E.g. the shareholders in a statutory audit

* Subject matter: This is the data such as the financial statements that have been prepared by the responsible party for the practitioner to evaluate. Another example might be a cash flow forecast to be reviewed by the practitioner.

* Suitable criteria: This can be thought of as ‘the rules’ against which the subject matter is evaluated in order to reach an opinion. In a statutory audit this would be the applicable reporting framework (e.g. IFRS and company law).
* Evidence: Information used by the practitioner in arriving at the conclusion on which their opinion is based. This must be sufficient (enough) and appropriate (relevant).
* Assurance Report: The report (normally written) containing the practitioner’s opinion. This is issued to the intended user following the collection of evidence.

sections.title: 2.4 Objectives of an assurance engagement
paragraphs: The primary objective of an assurance engagement is to enhance the degree of confidence of the intended users in the subject matter information. The practitioner achieves this by gathering sufficient appropriate evidence and expressing a written conclusion about the subject matter's conformity with identified criteria.

Beyond this overall purpose, specific assurance engagements may have more tailored objectives. For instance, an assurance engagement could be designed to conclude on the effectiveness of internal controls, compliance with laws or regulations, sustainability metrics, or other subject matters. In each case, however, the common objective is to have a qualified independent practitioner evaluate the subject matter against suitable criteria and provide a conclusion (often in the form of an opinion or report) that lends credibility to the subject matter information.

sections.title: 2.5 Phases of an Assurance Engagement
paragraphs: Assurance engagements, much like audits, typically progress through a series of phases or stages. Each phase has specific activities and objectives, ensuring a systematic approach to achieving the engagement’s overall goal. While the exact terminology may vary, we can summarize the phases as follows:
| **Phase** | **Description** |

| **1. Acceptance and Continuance** | The practitioner ensures that all preconditions are met (e.g., suitable subject matter, criteria, evidence, and independence). If any requirement is missing, the engagement is declined. |
| **2. Planning** | An overall audit strategy and detailed plan are developed. Risks are assessed and materiality is determined to guide resource allocation and audit procedures. |

| **3. Execution (Evidence Gathering)** | Assurance procedures such as inspections, confirmations, and analytical procedures are performed. The plan may be updated as necessary, and professional scepticism is maintained throughout the engagement. |

| **4. Evaluation and Conclusion** | Evidence is evaluated against the criteria, considering both materiality and qualitative factors. Any inconsistencies are resolved before forming the conclusion. |
| **5. Reporting** | An assurance report is issued describing the scope, responsibilities, and conclusion. The report clearly distinguishes the engagement from non-assurance engagements. |

sections.title: 2.6 Types of Assurance Engagements
paragraphs: The IAASB’s assurance framework recognises several types or categories of assurance engagements, which can be distinguished by their subject matters, their structure, or the standards under which they are performed. Here we discuss a few ways to classify assurance engagements:
* Attestation Engagement (Assertion-Based)
In this type, the responsible party (e.g., management) evaluates the subject matter and provides subject matter information (like financial statements).
The practitioner’s role is to assess and attest whether this information is reliable.
An audit is a common example: management prepares the statements, and the auditor expresses an opinion on them. The report refers to the assertion made by the responsible party.
* Direct Engagement (Direct Reporting)

Here, the practitioner directly evaluates the subject matter without a prior assertion from management. They both measure and report the outcome (e.g., effectiveness of internal controls). The report includes the subject matter information and the conclusion. This type is often used in non-financial assurance like compliance audits.

sections.title: 2.7 Non-Assurance Engagements:
paragraphs: The IAASB clearly distinguishes assurance engagements from other professional services where no assurance is expressed. While assurance engagements aim to provide a conclusion that enhances the confidence of users (such as audits and reviews), many services provided by accountants fall under non-assurance engagements, which include compilation, valuation, and recruitment services. These services do not meet the criteria for assurance under the IAASB framework and are often classified as advisory, consulting, or related services.
* Preparing Accounting Records and Financial Statements

This involves assisting management in compiling financial information such as maintaining ledgers, posting journal entries, or drafting financial statements. Governed by ISRS 4410, this is a related service, not an assurance engagement. The practitioner does not provide a conclusion or opinion on the accuracy of the information. Instead, the client is responsible for the financial information's reliability. The compilation report clearly states that no assurance is provided. However, when the same firm is also the auditor, ethical concerns may arise due to the self-review threat.
* Valuation Services

Valuation involves estimating the worth of assets, liabilities, or businesses for example, in mergers, fair value accounting, or asset impairments. While it may appear similar to assurance, valuation is an expert or consulting service, not an assurance engagement, because it does not involve evaluating information against a formal set of criteria for external users. The accountant provides management with a valuation report, not an assurance conclusion. If, however, an accountant were to audit a fair value prepared by someone else, that may constitute an assurance task. Ethical and independence rules still apply if the valuation is done for an audit client.
* Recruitment Services

Recruitment services involve helping clients hire staff—for example, screening candidates or assisting in executive hiring. These are clearly consulting services, not assurance engagements, as they do not involve verifying information or issuing a conclusion. The practitioner provides advice, not confidence to third-party users. However, ethical issues arise if such services are provided to audit clients, especially in hiring senior management, as it may create self-interest or familiarity threats.

In conclusion, these non-assurance services differ fundamentally from assurance engagements because they do not include the expression of an independent conclusion intended to instil confidence in users. While professional standards and ethical requirements still apply, such engagements fall outside the scope of IAASB assurance standards. Understanding these distinctions is important for proper application of standards and maintaining independence in practice.

sections.title: 3.1 Material Misstatement: Concept and Impact on Assurance
paragraphs: A material misstatement occurs when subject matter information (like financial statements or sustainability reports) deviates from the applicable criteria in a way that could reasonably influence user decisions. Misstatements can arise from errors or fraud and may be quantitative or qualitative.
* Planning: Practitioners set materiality thresholds early to determine the focus areas. These thresholds depend on user needs and engagement context.
* Risk Assessment: Includes identifying inherent and control risks in high-judgment or error-prone areas (e.g., revenue recognition).
* Detection and Evaluation: Practitioners detect misstatements during evidence gathering. If material, they may extend procedures to assess full impact.
* Correction and Reporting: Misstatements are discussed with management. If uncorrected and material, they affect the audit opinion leading to qualified or adverse opinions.
* Communication: All significant misstatements, corrected or uncorrected, are communicated to management or governance bodies.

sections.title: 3.2 Traditional Assurance Procedures
paragraphs: Traditional assurance procedures include:
| **Procedure** | **Explanation** |
| **Inquiry** | Asking questions from informed personnel. |
| **Inspection** | Examining documents or physical assets. |
| **Observation** | Watching processes or procedures being performed. |
| **Recalculation** | Verifying the arithmetic accuracy of calculations. |
| **Reperformance** | Independently repeating procedures performed by responsible parties. |
| **Analytical Procedures** | Studying trends, ratios, and applying mathematical reasoning. |
| **External Confirmation** | Obtaining independent verification from third parties. |

sections.title: 3.3 Automated Tools and Techniques (ATT)
paragraphs: Automated Tools and Techniques (ATT) refer to the use of technology to perform assurance procedures traditionally done manually. The IAASB defines ATT broadly, covering everything from data analytics to AI, RPA (Robotic Process Automation), and even drones.
Types and Uses of ATT
ATT can range from basic (e.g., using Excel) to advanced tools like:
* Generalized Audit Software (GAS) for sorting and analyzing entire data sets.
* Data Analytics to detect anomalies and trends across full populations.
* Process Mining and Risk Visualization for identifying control weaknesses or risks.
* Digital Confirmations to streamline third-party evidence gathering.
* RPA and AI to automate repetitive tasks or analyze contracts and transactions.
* Drones for inventory observation in inaccessible locations.
Benefits of ATT
* Full-population testing increases coverage compared to sampling.
* Anomaly detection through pattern recognition can highlight risks.
* Efficiency improves by automating routine procedures.
* Integrated procedures (e.g., a single analytic tool may serve as a risk assessment, analytical, and substantive procedure).
* Handling big/unstructured data that manual methods can’t easily process.
Challenges and Considerations
Practitioner must ensure:
* Reliability of the tool (verify code, scripts, or algorithms used)
* Data integrity (ensure complete and unaltered client data).
* Professional interpretation of the outcome
* Proper documentation of the procedures and results from ATT

sections.title: 3.4 Quality of evidence and its impact on conclusions
paragraphs: In assurance engagements, practitioners rely on sufficient appropriate evidence, which must be both relevant (logically connected to the assertion) and reliable (from trustworthy sources). When evidence is consistent, forming a conclusion is relatively straightforward. However, when contradictory evidence arises, it can complicate professional judgment and decision-making.

Contradictory evidence occurs when one piece of evidence supports a conclusion, while another suggests the opposite. For example, if customer confirmations agree with receivable balances but internal ledgers show discrepancies, the auditor cannot immediately conclude that receivables are fairly stated. Instead, they must investigate the inconsistency.
Such contradictions require the practitioner to:
* Weigh the reliability of each piece (e.g., external vs. internal sources).
* Perform additional procedures to resolve inconsistencies.
* Avoid relying on incomplete or biased evidence, even if voluminous.
* Adjust the risk assessment, strategy, or conclusion if the contradiction cannot be resolved.

If unresolved, contradictory evidence may lead the practitioner to modify the opinion, expand testing, or withhold assurance. For example, if a lawyer’s letter discloses litigation that management denies, this indicates a possible misstatement or management bias, requiring deeper inquiry.

Ultimately, conclusions must be well-reasoned and evidence-based. If multiple sources corroborate a finding, confidence increases. If evidence conflicts, the practitioner must either resolve the conflict or reflect the uncertainty in their report, ensuring that users are not misled by an overconfident conclusion. Thus, handling contradictory evidence is a critical test of professional scepticism and judgment in any assurance engagement.

sections.title: 3.5 Connected Information
paragraphs: Connected information refers to data or evidence that links various elements of the subject matter being examined. In assurance engagements, this interconnectedness helps practitioners form a more complete, coherent understanding of the entity’s operations, risks, and controls.

When information from different sources (e.g., financial, operational, regulatory) aligns, it enhances the reliability and persuasiveness of evidence. For example, if sales data, bank deposits, and tax filings all support recorded revenue, the assurance gained is stronger.

Conversely, inconsistencies or gaps in connected information can signal errors, fraud, or control weaknesses, prompting further investigation. For instance, if payroll records and HR reports do not reconcile, it may suggest fictitious employees or poor controls.

Connected information also aids in risk assessment, helping practitioners identify unusual patterns or anomalies across systems. It enables cross-validation of evidence and supports more informed judgments and conclusions.

Using connected information helps the practitioner see the full picture and understand how different parts of the business relate to each other. This leads to better, more accurate assurance and gives more confidence in the assurance report.

sections.title: 3.6 Sufficiency, Appropriateness, and Documentation
paragraphs: In an assurance engagement, the auditor must obtain and document sufficient appropriate evidence to support their conclusion. This means the evidence must be enough in quantity (sufficiency) and high in quality (appropriateness, which includes relevance and reliability).
To assess whether this has been achieved, the practitioner considers:
* Coverage: Have all significant areas, risks, and assertions been addressed through relevant procedures?
* Reliability: Has the evidence come from dependable sources (e.g., third parties, direct observation, or recalculation)?
* Relevance: Does the evidence directly relate to the specific assertion or subject matter?
* Consistency: Does the evidence from various procedures and sources support the same conclusion, or are there contradictions that require further work?
In an assurance engagement, proper documentation is much critical. The practitioner must clearly record:
* What procedures were performed
* What evidence was obtained
* How that evidence supports the conclusion

The documentation should be clear and complete so that another experienced assurance practitioner could understand the nature, timing, and extent of the procedures, and how the evidence led to the conclusion. If documentation is lacking or unclear, it may appear that the assurance conclusion is not well supported, even if sufficient appropriate evidence was actually gathered. Therefore, thorough documentation is just as important as obtaining the evidence itself in delivering a credible assurance engagement

sections.title: 4.1 Requirements of Sustainability Reporting in an Audit Context
paragraphs: Sustainability reporting discloses an organization’s environmental, social, and governance (ESG) performance, using frameworks such as IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards (S1 & S2) and Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards. These frameworks ensure transparency, comparability, and completeness, forming the foundation for audit and assurance engagements.
For sustainability information to be assured, certain preconditions must be met:
* Use of recognised reporting frameworks (GRI, IFRS S1/S2, SASB).
* Disclosure of material ESG matters that significantly impact stakeholders.
* Compliance with regulatory requirements (e.g., Code of Corporate Governance).

Auditors assess sustainability information against suitable criteria, ensuring it is verifiable, measurable, and reliable. Like financial audits, sustainability reporting requires proper documentation to support assurance engagements.

sections.title: 4.2 Disclosures in Assurance of Sustainability Reporting
paragraphs: Assurance reports must clearly communicate the results of engagements to enhance stakeholder confidence. Key disclosures include:
* Subject matter (specific sustainability metrics or entire reports).
* Criteria/framework used (GRI, IFRS S1/S2).
* Level of assurance (reasonable or limited).
* Scope of work and procedures performed.
* Responsibilities of management and the assurance provider.

Reports must state the standards followed (e.g. ISAE 3000 or ISSA 5000) and declare the practitioner’s independence. Any limitations (e.g., exclusions of certain data) must be disclosed to ensure transparency.

sections.title: 4.3 Ethical Considerations for Assurance of Sustainability Reporting
paragraphs: The International Ethics Standards for Sustainability Assurance (IESSA) introduces the need for ethical consideration in such engagements by following words:

“It is of public interest that sustainability assurance practitioners act ethically in order to maintain public trust and confidence in sustainability information that is subject to assurance. High-quality ethics and independence standards alongside other reporting and assurance standards will help investors, customers, employees and other users of sustainability information to confidently rely on such information in their decision-making (5100.1)”

“Sustainability assurance practitioners are expected to have relevant knowledge, skills and experience to perform sustainability assurance engagements and have appropriate training to ensure their assurance skills are continually up to date with relevant developments (5100.1a)”
The fundamental principles are:
a) Integrity – to be straightforward and honest in all professional and business relationships.
b) Objectivity – to not allow bias, conflict of interest or undue influence of others to override professional or business judgments.
c) Professional Competence and Due Care – to:

- Attain and maintain professional knowledge and skill at the level required to ensure that a sustainability assurance client receives competent professional service, based on current technical and professional standards and relevant legislation; and
- Act diligently and in accordance with applicable technical and professional standards.
d) Confidentiality – to respect the confidentiality of information acquired as a result of professional and business relationships.

e) Professional Behaviour – to comply with relevant laws and regulations and avoid any conduct that the sustainability assurance practitioner knows or should know might discredit the sustainability assurance profession
High ethical standards (e.g., IESBA Code of Ethics) protect public trust in sustainability reporting.

sections.title: 4.4 Examples of Sustainability Reporting in an Audit Context
paragraphs: 1. Framework-Based Reporting and Assurance

Company A publishes a sustainability report using GRI Standards, covering carbon emissions, energy usage, and workforce data. An external auditor performs limited assurance and verifies data against GRI criteria using ISAE 3000/ISSA 5000. The assurance statement confirms no material misstatements, reinforcing stakeholder trust.
2. Mandatory Sustainability Reporting with Audit Oversight

Company B operates in a jurisdiction requiring integrated financial and sustainability reporting. Auditors review sustainability disclosures for material inconsistencies under ISA 720 (Other Information). Additionally, greenhouse gas emissions are independently verified under ISO 14064 standards, ensuring compliance and strengthening investor confidence.
3. Ethical Independence in Assurance Services

Company C, a tech firm, seeks sustainability assurance after hiring a consultancy to develop its ESG data systems. Its audit firm identifies a self-review threat and assigns a separate assurance team to maintain independence. The assurance report follows IESBA’s Code of Ethics, explicitly confirming independence and ethical compliance.
4. Reasonable Assurance on Key ESG Metrics

Company D requires reasonable assurance on workplace safety and GHG emissions, key ESG metrics for investors and regulators. The assurance provider conducts in-depth testing (inspections, data verification, and recalculations) and issues a positive opinion, demonstrating the rigor and reliability of ESG reporting.
5. Public Accountability in Sustainability Assurance

Company E, a state-owned enterprise, must report national sustainability commitments (e.g., emissions reduction). An independent auditor provides reasonable assurance, addressing the report to management, regulators, and public stakeholders. This ensures full transparency and reinforces public trust.

sections.title: 5.1 Introduction to Sustainability Assurance Engagements
paragraphs: The scope of the assurance engagement may extend to all of the sustainability information to be reported by the entity or only part of that information. For example, some jurisdictions may require the entirety of the sustainability information to be reported in accordance with an established framework to be subject to assurance. However, in certain jurisdictions, law or regulation may require that only climate-related disclosures in an entity’s sustainability information be subject to assurance. The reporting requirements of this ISSA require the practitioner to identify or describe the information that is subject to the assurance engagement

Sustainability assurance engagements independently evaluate ESG disclosures, ensuring transparency, reliability, and accountability. These engagements align sustainability assurance with financial audits, following standards like ISAE 3000 and IAASB’s ISSA 5000.

sections.title: Levels of Assurance
paragraphs: * Limited assurance – Moderate confidence (e.g. no evidence of misstatements found).
* Reasonable assurance – Higher confidence (e.g. ESG information is fairly stated).
IAASB ISSA 5000 provides a global framework for sustainability assurance, ensuring consistent, high-quality engagements across industries.

sections.title: 5.2 Objectives of Sustainability Assurance Engagements
paragraphs: Sustainability information is reported in accordance with the criteria. This ISSA requires the practitioner to evaluate whether the criteria that the practitioner expects to be applied in the preparation of the sustainability information are suitable for the engagement circumstances. In the absence of indications to the contrary, framework criteria that are embodied in law or regulation or are established by authorized or recognised organizations that follow a transparent due process are presumed to be suitable
The objectives of the practitioner are to:
* obtain either reasonable assurance or limited assurance, as appropriate, about whether the sustainability information is free from material misstatement

* express a conclusion on the sustainability information through a written report that conveys a reasonable assurance or a limited assurance conclusion, as applicable, and describes the basis for the conclusion
* communicate further as required by this ISSA and any other relevant ISSA.

In all cases when reasonable assurance or limited assurance, as applicable, cannot be obtained and a qualified conclusion in the practitioner’s assurance report is insufficient in the circumstances for purposes of reporting to the intended users, this ISSA requires the practitioner to disclaim a conclusion or withdraw from the engagement, where withdrawal is possible under applicable law or regulation.

sections.title: 5.3 Elements of Sustainability Assurance Engagements
paragraphs: | **Element** | **Description** |

| **1. Three-party Relationship** | The assurance engagement involves:
- Practitioner (e.g., auditor or assurance provider)
- Responsible party (usually management of the entity)
- Intended users (stakeholders relying on the sustainability information). |

| **2. Appropriate Underlying Subject Matter** | The subject matter must be identifiable and capable of consistent evaluation or measurement against the criteria. In sustainability engagements, this typically includes ESG disclosures, carbon emissions, supply chain impacts, etc. |

| **3. Suitable Criteria** | The subject matter must be evaluated against established benchmarks (e.g., GRI, SASB, IFRS S1/S2, or laws/regulations). Criteria must be relevant, complete, reliable, neutral, and understandable. |

| **4. Sufficient Appropriate Evidence** | The practitioner must obtain enough relevant and reliable evidence to support their conclusion. The level of evidence differs between reasonable and limited assurance engagements. |

| **5. Assurance Report** | A written report expressing the conclusion in a clear and unambiguous manner. It must specify the level of assurance and describe the basis of conclusion, scope, and any limitations. |

sections.title: 5.4 Examples of Sustainability Assurance Engagements and IAASB ISSA 5000
paragraphs: 1. Social Compliance Audit in Supply Chains Company: Clothing Retailer Q

Scenario: Q sources products from global factories and has committed to fair wages, no child labour, and safe working conditions. To ensure credibility, Q hires an assurance provider to validate its supplier compliance claims.
Key Assurance Elements:
* Subject Matter: Supplier compliance with Q’s Labor Code of Conduct.
* Criteria: Labor standards used in audits
* Evidence Gathering: Factory site visits, worker interviews, inspection of internal audit reports.
* Assurance Level: Limited assurance (sampling-based assessment).
* Outcome: The assurance report confirms compliance levels, reinforcing stakeholder confidence in ethical sourcing and meeting regulatory/import requirements in certain markets.
2. Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions Verification for Carbon Trading Company: ABC Manufacturing
Scenario: ABC requires independent assurance on its Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions for regulatory compliance and carbon trading eligibility.
Key Assurance Elements:
* Subject Matter: Reported GHG emissions for the year.
* Criteria: Greenhouse Gas Protocol, ISO 14064 for emissions measurement.
* Evidence Gathering: Verification of energy bills, recalculations of emission factors, third-party certification records.
* Assurance Level: Reasonable assurance due to financial and compliance implications.
* Outcome: The assurance report confirms emissions accuracy, ensuring credibility for climate reporting and carbon credit transactions.
3. Assurance for Sustainability-Linked Bonds Company: Green Energy Co.

Scenario: Green Energy Co. issued a sustainability-linked bond, where the interest rate is tied to achieving renewable energy goals. An independent assurance provider is engaged to verify whether the company met its agreed sustainability targets.
Key Assurance Elements:
* Subject Matter: Reported renewable energy usage percentage.
* Criteria: Industry standards & bond agreement definitions of renewable energy.
* Evidence Gathering: Energy purchase records, contract verifications, supplier certifications.
* Assurance Level: Reasonable assurance due to financial implications.
* Outcome: The assurance statement, addressed to both management and bondholders, confirms whether Green Energy met its target, impacting the interest rate paid on the bond.
4. Community & NGO Assurance on Environmental and Social Impact Company: ABC Mining

Scenario: Due to community concerns over its environmental and social impact, ABC Mining agrees with NGOs to undergo independent sustainability assurance, ensuring transparency in land restoration and community engagement claims.
Key Assurance Elements:
* Subject Matter: Sustainability report sections on land rehabilitation, water quality, and community grievance resolution.
* Criteria: Local environmental laws, voluntary sustainability frameworks, and company commitments.
* Evidence Gathering: Field inspections, laboratory water quality tests, community feedback analysis.
* Assurance Level: Limited assurance, with an NGO-involved review process.
* Outcome: The assurance report, made public, builds trust between ABC Mining, NGOs, and local communities, ensuring sustainability claims are independently verified.
5. Multi-Framework Assurance Under ISSA 5000 Company: Global Conglomerate Y

Scenario: Global Y reports sustainability data under multiple frameworks (GRI, TCFD, SASB) to meet diverse stakeholder needs. To ensure global consistency, the company aligns all sustainability assurance engagements with ISSA 5000.
Key Assurance Elements:
* Subject Matter: Full sustainability report, covering climate risks, social impact, and governance metrics.
* Criteria: GRI, TCFD, and SASB standards.
* Evidence Gathering: Governance reviews, site inspections, verification of climate risk disclosures.
* Assurance Level: Limited assurance for qualitative disclosures, reasonable assurance for quantitative ESG metrics.
* Outcome: The assurance statement references ISSA 5000, ensuring that ESG disclosures meet an internationally recognised assurance standard, increasing investor and regulatory confidence.
